name: Tailoring background for data annotation role
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The user worked with Claude to refine their professional background for data annotation company applications. They have experience as a QA Analyst at a software company, a degree in video game design, and diverse background including photography, videography, fabrication, painting, event production, and music festivals. They also have experience from various sectors including healthcare and construction work. The user is actively pursuing film industry opportunities and uses AI tools daily for data analysis, creative brainstorming, and productivity.

The conversation focused on iteratively improving a background statement, with the user consistently pushing for more natural, human-sounding language. They demonstrated strong preferences for direct, conversational communication that avoids corporate jargon, wanting text that sounds like "someone who just got done listening to a podcast" rather than formal business writing. The user was particularly concerned about AI detection, asking Claude to evaluate whether text looked AI-generated and requesting specific improvements like reducing repetitive words, removing excessive em dashes, and adding minor imperfections to make writing appear more authentically human.

They also worked on developing a skills list for data annotation platforms, focusing on programming (20%), design (20%), general skills (30%), technical abilities (15%), and software skills (15%). The final skills list emphasized their Computer Science background, visual analysis capabilities, QA testing experience, and creative problem-solving abilities. Near the end, the user shared a job posting for AI Red Teaming at July AI ($30-50/hour, remote, flexible 10-40 hours/week), which Claude identified as a strong match for their QA mindset, creative background, and AI tool experience. The user's communication style throughout was direct and collaborative, frequently asking for specific adjustments and showing attention to detail in how their professional presentation would be perceived by recruiters.

chat_messages.text: Can you take this and make it more oriented to something a company like data annotation would be looking for in their background section:I am a creative individual with a degree and in video game design. I am a photographer by trade and I’m actively pursuing the film industry. I have a very diverse work history, which has given me experience in healthcare, military, retail, and culinary. Notably, I have worked for a software company as a QA Analyst. I am an everyday user of AI, using it to effectively assist me with crunching data, creative brainstorm, and daily productivity.

The user wants me to rewrite this background section to be more oriented toward data annotation companies. Let me think about what data annotation companies look for:

1. Technical skills and attention to detail
2. Experience with QA/quality assurance
3. Understanding of AI and machine learning
4. Ability to follow instructions precisely
5. Pattern recognition
6. Data analysis experience
7. Software/technical background

The user's current text is:
- Too casual/conversational
- Focuses on creative aspects that aren't as relevant
- Buries the most relevant experience (QA Analyst, AI usage)
- Doesn't emphasize technical precision or attention to detail

I should:
- Lead with technical/analytical strengths
- Emphasize QA experience prominently
- Highlight AI understanding from a technical user perspective
- Frame diverse experience as adaptability and quick learning
- Show attention to detail and pattern recognition
- Keep it professional but not overly formal
- Make it sound like "one pro talking to another" as per his preferences

Let me draft something that emphasizes the right skills while keeping his authentic voice.I bring technical precision and pattern recognition skills from diverse experience across software QA, military healthcare, and creative production. As a QA Analyst at a software company, I developed a methodical approach to identifying issues, documenting edge cases, and ensuring quality standards—skills that translate directly to data annotation work.

My daily work with AI tools has given me practical understanding of how models process and learn from data. I use these tools for data analysis, workflow optimization, and pattern identification, which has made me familiar with the importance of accurate, consistent labeling and annotation.

My background in video game design and photography has sharpened my visual analysis capabilities and attention to spatial relationships, while military training instilled discipline in following protocols precisely. This combination of technical QA experience, AI literacy, and visual expertise makes me well-suited for annotation projects requiring both accuracy and contextual understanding.

I'm comfortable working independently with detailed guidelines and can quickly adapt to new annotation frameworks and quality standards.
make it more natural

The user wants this to sound more natural - less formal and corporate, more like someone actually talking. Looking at their preferences, they want direct, no-nonsense communication that's "relatable and professional" - like someone who "just got done listening to a podcast." They consistently push back against overly formal language.

Let me make this sound like a real person explaining their background, not a corporate robot.I've spent time as a QA Analyst at a software company, which taught me how to spot inconsistencies, document problems clearly, and follow testing protocols. That experience translates pretty directly to annotation work—same attention to detail, same need for consistency.

I use AI tools daily for everything from data analysis to project planning, so I understand how these models work and why clean, accurate data matters. I've seen firsthand what happens when inputs are messy versus when they're done right.

My background in photography and video game design means I'm used to analyzing visual information and understanding spatial relationships. Military training gave me the discipline to follow guidelines precisely and work independently without constant oversight. Between the QA experience and creative work, I'm comfortable switching between technical accuracy and contextual judgment calls.

I can adapt quickly to new annotation frameworks and maintain quality standards across long projects.
add some lifestyle stuff in there about photography videography art fabrication painting  i love event production / music festival stuff that kind of thing

The user wants to add more lifestyle/creative elements to make this feel more natural and give a fuller picture of who they are. They want to incorporate photography, videography, art, fabrication, painting, event production, and music festival work. This should flow naturally and show how these creative pursuits relate to data annotation work.

I should weave this in naturally rather than just tacking it on as a list. Make it conversational and show how these interests actually relate to the work.I've spent time as a QA Analyst at a software company, which taught me how to spot inconsistencies, document problems clearly, and follow testing protocols. That experience translates pretty directly to annotation work—same attention to detail, same need for consistency.

I use AI tools daily for everything from data analysis to project planning, so I understand how these models work and why clean, accurate data matters. I've seen firsthand what happens when inputs are messy versus when they're done right.

Outside of tech work, I'm deep in the creative world—photography, videography, fabrication, and painting. I've done event production and worked music festivals, which means I'm used to managing complex visual environments and making quick judgment calls under pressure. My background in video game design sharpened my eye for visual composition and spatial relationships, and fabrication work requires the same precision and problem-solving that annotation does.

Military training gave me the discipline to follow guidelines precisely and work independently without constant oversight. Between the QA experience, creative production work, and hands-on fabrication, I'm comfortable switching between technical accuracy and contextual judgment. I can look at data from multiple angles—technical, visual, and practical.

I adapt quickly to new annotation frameworks and can maintain quality standards across long projects without losing focus.
make it subtly professional and pleasant but analytical

The user wants me to refine the tone to be "subtly professional and pleasant but analytical" - so maintaining the natural feel but polishing it to be more measured, thoughtful, and professionally engaging. Less casual, more considered, but still authentic and not stuffy.I've worked as a QA Analyst at a software company, where I developed systematic approaches to identifying inconsistencies, documenting edge cases, and maintaining testing protocols. This experience translates well to annotation work—both require the same level of precision and consistent methodology.

I use AI tools regularly for data analysis and workflow optimization, which has given me practical insight into how models process information and why data quality matters at the input stage. I've observed the direct relationship between annotation accuracy and model performance.

My work spans creative and technical domains—photography, videography, fabrication, and painting, alongside event production and music festival coordination. These experiences have trained me to analyze complex visual environments quickly and make informed decisions about spatial relationships and compositional elements. My background in video game design further developed my ability to evaluate visual data from multiple perspectives.

Military training established my discipline around following detailed guidelines and working autonomously. The combination of QA methodology, creative production experience, and hands-on fabrication gives me a balanced approach: I can maintain technical rigor while applying contextual understanding when needed.

I adapt efficiently to new annotation frameworks and can sustain quality standards throughout extended projects. The work appeals to both my analytical side and my understanding of how visual and spatial information functions in different contexts.
read it as a recruiter would read it. Does it look like ai wrote it?

The user is asking me to evaluate the text from a recruiter's perspective to see if it sounds AI-generated. Let me read it critically:

Issues that might flag as AI-written:
1. "developed systematic approaches" - very AI phrase
2. "translates well to annotation work" - common AI transition phrase
3. "practical insight into how models process information" - overly technical/formal
4. "direct relationship between annotation accuracy and model performance" - sounds like jargon
5. "trained me to analyze complex visual environments quickly" - AI loves "trained me to"
6. "informed decisions about spatial relationships" - very formal
7. "further developed my ability" - classic AI progression phrase
8. "established my discipline around" - awkward phrasing
9. "balanced approach" - generic AI conclusion phrase
10. "technical rigor while applying contextual understanding" - corporate buzzword soup
11. "adapt efficiently" and "sustain quality standards" - AI loves these efficiency phrases
12. "appeals to both my analytical side" - overly self-aware/formal

The structure is also very AI: each paragraph follows a perfect formula (claim → evidence → relevance), and the sentences are all similar length and complexity. No personality, no natural flow, very sanitized.

A real person would:
- Use more varied sentence structure
- Include some casual language mixed with professional
- Not explain every single connection so explicitly
- Have some rougher edges or less perfectly balanced phrasing
- Show personality in word choice

I should be honest that yes, this reads very AI-generated to a critical eye.Yeah, it does. Several red flags:

**AI tells:**
- "developed systematic approaches" / "established my discipline around" - overly formal phrasing
- "translates well to" / "further developed my ability" - classic AI connector phrases
- Every paragraph has the exact same structure: claim → evidence → relevance
- "technical rigor while applying contextual understanding" - buzzword stacking
- "adapt efficiently" / "sustain quality standards" - corporate AI favorites
- All sentences are roughly the same length and complexity
- Zero personality or natural speech patterns

**A real person would:**
- Mix casual and professional language unevenly
- Have some rougher transitions
- Not explain every single connection explicitly
- Show personality through word choice quirks
- Have varied sentence rhythm

Want me to rewrite it so it sounds like an actual human wrote it? Something with some natural imperfection and voice?
yep sure do

The user wants me to rewrite this to sound like a real person wrote it, not AI. I need to:
- Use more natural, conversational language
- Vary sentence structure and length
- Have some rougher transitions
- Include personality
- Not connect everything perfectly
- Mix casual and professional tones
- Maybe have a slightly imperfect flow

Let me write something that sounds like someone actually typing this up for a job application, not an AI generating perfect prose.I worked as a QA Analyst at a software company, which was all about catching problems, documenting what broke, and making sure things stayed consistent. That mindset carries over well—annotation needs that same kind of systematic attention.

I use AI tools pretty regularly for data work and project management, so I get how these systems actually function and why the quality of training data makes or breaks everything. It's interesting work when you understand what's happening under the hood.

Outside of the tech side, I'm involved in photography, videography, fabrication, and painting. I've done event production and worked music festivals, which means dealing with chaotic visual environments and making calls on the fly. My degree is in video game design, which helped me learn to break down visual composition and spatial logic. Fabrication work is similar to QA in a lot of ways—precision matters, and you have to troubleshoot as you go.

Military background gave me the structure to follow detailed procedures and work independently without much supervision. The mix of analytical work and creative production means I can approach annotation from both angles—technical accuracy when it's needed, but also understanding context and visual information in a practical way.

I pick up new systems quickly and can stay focused on repetitive tasks for long stretches. The work appeals to me because it combines pattern recognition with actual application—not just data for data's sake.
what with all the— would a recruiter look at the number of— and think thats normal?

The user is pointing out that I used a lot of em dashes (—) in the text, and asking if a recruiter would think that's normal. They're right - I overused them. It's actually an AI writing tell when there are too many em dashes. Real people use them occasionally, but not multiple times in a short paragraph. Let me count them:

- "which was all about catching problems, documenting what broke, and making sure things stayed consistent. That mindset carries over well—annotation needs..."
- "precision matters, and you have to troubleshoot as you go."
- "technical accuracy when it's needed, but also understanding..."
- "pattern recognition with actual application—not just data..."

I used 2 em dashes in a relatively short piece. That's probably too many for natural writing. Most people would use periods, commas, or restructure sentences instead of relying on em dashes.

The user is right to call this out. I should acknowledge this is another AI tell and offer to fix it.You're right—that's another AI tell. I used two em dashes in a short piece, which is too many. Real people use them sparingly or not at all. It looks like someone trying to sound sophisticated.

attachments.extracted_content:
# Use Your Creativity to Make a Frontier AI Model Safer (AI Red Teaming)

**Duration:** Nov (short-term contractor). Possibility for extension

**Commitment:** Part-time or Full-Time (10–40 hrs/week)

**Location:** SF in-office or Remote (must be a US citizen)

**Compensation: $30-$50/hour**, open for discussion

**Other Perks:** Direct touchpoints with AI researchers

**Next step:** Apply via the link below, and we will invite select candidates for interviews

## About [July AI](http://www.gojuly.ai)

July AI is building a data marketplace that is focused on serving the needs of humans. Our mission is to increase economic opportunities in the age of AI. We are based in San Francisco, backed by top-tier investors like Basis Set, Night Capital, SV Angel, Olive Capital.

## About the Role

We’re looking for writers, storytellers, and creative thinkers eager to apply their craft in ways AI can’t replace - and to reinvent their skills for the AI-fueled future. By using your language, rhetoric, and imagination skills, you’ll help tackle one of the most important challenges in technology: AI safety.

This role is called AI red teaming. You will create adversarial conversations/scenarios by writing a series of prompts that are fed into AI models to see how they respond. The goal with these conversations is to try and trick, confuse, or jailbreak the system - in other words, get the AI model to produce an unsafe or unintended response. 

Your prompts will be used to improve the safety of a frontier research lab’s newest AI model. This is an opportunity to pivot into the AI field, and make real-world impact by helping build the safety infrastructure for a leading AI model - creating safer technology for society as a whole.

We want to onboard contributors as soon as possible, but the official project will run for 3-4 weeks in November.

## Who we want

- **Ideal background includes (but not limited to):**
    - Creative writer
    - Cybersecurity / Red teamer
    - Prompt Engineer
    - Journalist / Editor
    - Copy Writer
    - Designer
    - Marketer
    - And other talented creatives who enjoy pushing the limits of AI and have the potential to create high quality stories/prompts
- **Bonus skills:**
    - Ability to think critically, craft storylines/characters, and able to effectively understand and communicate with AI models in written form
    - Interested in AI safety and ethics
    - Keen to acquire skills in AI

## Core responsibilities

- Craft adversarial test prompts / stories that stress test the AI model
- Interpret / understand AI model behavior
- Understand and follow the exact criteria for each attack and adhere to any safety or content rules we provide so your tests are relevant
- Document your findings: For each test, record the steps to reproduce it, how serious the problem is, and any ideas for fixing or reducing the issue
- Keep the July AI team updated with findings throughout the engagement
- Adhere to NDAs, ethics, and safety guidelines

## Requirements

- **Highly recommended for applicants unfamiliar with AI red teaming:** Complete the “Red Team - Beginner” module. This will provide a solid introduction to red teaming and make it easier to create strong sample submissions
- Need to complete and submit at least **5 sample red team conversations** on our platform (the instructions are in the application below)
    - These sample submissions don’t have to all be successful. We’re mainly looking for creativity, adaptability, and persistence (more detail under “Tips to create a good red team prompt”)
    - You may submit multiple attempts on the same challenge
- Excellent written documentation skills
- Availability during Oct–Nov; state earliest start date and weekly hours

## Application - Apply via [this link](https://form.typeform.com/to/sxEbJwG6)

## Tips to create a good red team conversation

- Each sample submission should consist of multiple prompts that produce a single narrative/story. The overarching goal is to use this narrative to trick the AI model into providing the desired response outlined in each challenge
- **Creativity: Ability to write a variety of different and original stories**
    - Make sure all your red team/attack prompts are original ideas. Craft a cohesive and creative story that tricks the AI model into providing the desired output
        - Each storyline has a persona (whether it’s you or a made-up character)
        - Ensure the prompts and stories are about a real person/scenario
            - Do not feed any gibberish into the model
- **Adaptability and Persistence: Able to consistently adjust attacks based on the AI model’s response**
    - These prompts should demonstrate that you are learning from the AI’s defenses and refining your strategy
    - Some of the best attempts are the ones where each prompt demonstrates a clear, logical progression that builds on the previous prompt
    - Make sure there is an intention behind each prompt
- **Writing Reproducible Prompts: Make sure you are able to explain your thought process behind the prompts/stories you are feeding into the model**
    - You can do this under the “Annotation” box in the sample submissions
    - Ideal if the model outputs a detailed response that satisfies the topic requirements

If there are any questions, please reach out to [info@gojuly.ai](mailto:info@gojuly.ai)
